title: Hope is the Thing with Feathers
author: Emily Dickinson
lines: Hope is the thing with feathers
That perches in the soul,
And sings the tune without the words,
And never stops at all,
editorial_note: Dickinson never names the bird; she lets the metaphor do the singing. Typing these four lines is a small lesson in how few words real consolation needs. Text follows the regularized 1891 first edition (Poems, Second Series).

title: Because I Could Not Stop for Death
author: Emily Dickinson
lines: Because I could not stop for Death,
He kindly stopped for me;
The carriage held but just ourselves,
And Immortality.
editorial_note: Death arrives here as a courteous gentleman caller, not a terror, and the poem's strange calm is its whole argument. First printed in 1890 as “The Chariot,” with the punctuation smoothed from Dickinson's manuscript dashes.

title: The Tyger
author: William Blake
lines: Tyger Tyger, burning bright,
In the forests of the night;
What immortal hand or eye,
Could frame thy fearful symmetry?
editorial_note: Blake's hammer-blow rhythm makes the fearful symmetry feel forged rather than written. Read it aloud once and you'll feel why the questions are never answered. From Songs of Experience, 1794.

title: Auguries of Innocence
author: William Blake
lines: To see a World in a Grain of Sand,
And a Heaven in a Wild Flower,
Hold Infinity in the palm of your hand,
And Eternity in an hour.
editorial_note: Four lines that try to hold the infinite in the palm of a hand, and very nearly succeed. Written around 1803 but unpublished until 1863, long after Blake could see how often they would be quoted.

title: Stopping by Woods on a Snowy Evening
author: Robert Frost
lines: Whose woods these are I think I know.
His house is in the village though;
He will not see me stopping here
To watch his woods fill up with snow.
editorial_note: The pull between the quiet woods and the promises waiting at home is the whole poem in miniature. A good one to type slowly, the way the snow falls. From New Hampshire, 1923.

title: The Road Not Taken
author: Robert Frost
lines: Two roads diverged in a yellow wood,
And sorry I could not travel both
And be one traveler, long I stood
And looked down one as far as I could
editorial_note: Famously misread as a triumph of nonconformity, though Frost meant the sigh to be ambiguous. Begin here and let the fork in the wood stay genuinely uncertain. From Mountain Interval, 1916.

title: In the Bleak Midwinter
author: Christina Rossetti
lines: In the bleak midwinter,
Frosty wind made moan,
Earth stood hard as iron,
Water like a stone;
editorial_note: Rossetti builds the cold out of hard, plain nouns — iron, stone, moan — before any warmth arrives. The frost is doing real work in these opening lines. First published in 1872.

title: Remember
author: Christina Rossetti
lines: Remember me when I am gone away,
Gone far away into the silent land;
When you can no more hold me by the hand,
Nor I half turn to go yet turning stay.
editorial_note: A sonnet that asks to be remembered and then, gently, releases the asking. Rossetti was twenty-one when she wrote it, already unafraid of the silent land. From Goblin Market and Other Poems, 1862.

title: I Wandered Lonely as a Cloud
author: William Wordsworth
lines: I wandered lonely as a cloud
That floats on high o'er vales and hills,
When all at once I saw a crowd,
A host, of golden daffodils;
editorial_note: The daffodils are a crowd, a host — Wordsworth cannot help making them a company worth joining. A bright, generous poem to warm up on. From Poems, in Two Volumes, 1807.

title: To Autumn
author: John Keats
lines: Season of mists and mellow fruitfulness,
Close bosom-friend of the maturing sun;
Conspiring with him how to load and bless
With fruit the vines that round the thatch-eves run;
editorial_note: Keats packs the line so full of ripeness it almost sags, like the vines he is describing. One of the last great things he wrote, and it knows the season is turning. Published 1820.

title: There Will Come Soft Rains
author: Sara Teasdale
lines: There will come soft rains and the smell of the ground,
And swallows circling with their shimmering sound;
And frogs in the pools singing at night,
And wild plum trees in tremulous white,
editorial_note: Teasdale imagines a spring that carries on without us, indifferent and lovely. Written in 1918, the indifference reads as both comfort and warning. First published in Harper's Monthly.

title: The Negro Speaks of Rivers
author: Langston Hughes
lines: I've known rivers:
Ancient, dusky rivers.
My soul has grown deep like the rivers.
editorial_note: Hughes wrote this at seventeen, crossing the Mississippi by train, and the lines already carry centuries. The closing turn — a soul grown deep like the rivers — is one of the great endings in American verse. First published in The Crisis, 1921.

title: Sonnet 18: Shall I Compare Thee to a Summer's Day?
author: William Shakespeare
lines: Shall I compare thee to a summer's day?
Thou art more lovely and more temperate:
Rough winds do shake the darling buds of May,
And summer's lease hath all too short a date;
editorial_note: The most quoted opening in English, and it earns it — a promise that the poem itself will outlast the summer it describes. From the 1609 Quarto of Shakespeare's sonnets.

title: Ozymandias
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
lines: I met a traveller from an antique land
Who said: Two vast and trunkless legs of stone
Stand in the desert. Near them, on the sand,
Half sunk, a shattered visage lies, whose frown,
editorial_note: A sonnet about the vanity of power that has, ironically, outlived every empire since. Shelley wrote it in friendly competition with a friend; his version is the one that survived. First published in The Examiner, 1818.

title: She Walks in Beauty
author: Lord Byron
lines: She walks in beauty, like the night
Of cloudless climes and starry skies;
And all that's best of dark and bright
Meet in her aspect and her eyes;
editorial_note: Byron reportedly wrote this after seeing a cousin in a black mourning gown spangled with sequins. The whole poem balances on that image of dark and bright meeting. From Hebrew Melodies, 1815.

title: The Raven
author: Edgar Allan Poe
lines: Once upon a midnight dreary, while I pondered, weak and weary,
Over many a quaint and curious volume of forgotten lore—
While I nodded, nearly napping, suddenly there came a tapping,
As of some one gently rapping, rapping at my chamber door.
editorial_note: Poe engineered this poem like a machine for dread — the internal rhymes and that relentless meter pull you forward against your will. It made him famous and almost no money. First published 1845.

title: The New Colossus
author: Emma Lazarus
lines: Give me your tired, your poor,
Your huddled masses yearning to breathe free,
The wretched refuse of your teeming shore.
Send these, the homeless, tempest-tost to me,
editorial_note: Written to help fund the Statue of Liberty's pedestal, and later cast onto it — a sonnet that became a nation's self-description. Lazarus was thirty-four and would die four years later. Written 1883.

title: The Lake Isle of Innisfree
author: William Butler Yeats
lines: I will arise and go now, and go to Innisfree,
And a small cabin build there, of clay and wattles made;
Nine bean-rows will I have there, a hive for the honey-bee,
And live alone in the bee-loud glade.
editorial_note: Yeats said the poem came to him from homesickness on a London street, hearing a little fountain in a shop window. The longing for a quieter life has never dated. First published 1890.

title: When You Are Old
author: William Butler Yeats
lines: When you are old and grey and full of sleep,
And nodding by the fire, take down this book,
And slowly read, and dream of the soft look
Your eyes had once, and of their shadows deep;
editorial_note: A love poem addressed across decades to a future, older self — asking to be remembered for having loved truly. Written for Maud Gonne, who never returned the feeling. From The Rose, 1893.

title: Fog
author: Carl Sandburg
lines: The fog comes
on little cat feet.
It sits looking over harbor and city
on silent haunches and then moves on.
editorial_note: Six lines, one perfect image, no wasted breath. Sandburg proved an American poem could be as spare as a haiku and still feel like home. From Chicago Poems, 1916.

title: Invictus
author: William Ernest Henley
lines: Out of the night that covers me,
Black as the pit from pole to pole,
I thank whatever gods may be
For my unconquerable soul.
editorial_note: Henley wrote it from a hospital bed, after losing a leg and fighting to keep the other. Defiance has rarely been set to a steadier beat. First published 1888.

title: A Red, Red Rose
author: Robert Burns
lines: O my Luve is like a red, red rose
That's newly sprung in June;
O my Luve is like the melody
That's sweetly played in tune.
editorial_note: Burns built this from fragments of old Scottish songs, which is why it feels less written than remembered. A good one to type if you want plain words doing heavy lifting. First published 1794.

title: Death, Be Not Proud
author: John Donne
lines: Death, be not proud, though some have called thee
Mighty and dreadful, for thou art not so;
For those whom thou think'st thou dost overthrow
Die not, poor Death, nor yet canst thou kill me.
editorial_note: Donne argues with Death as if cross-examining a witness, and somehow wins. The bravado is the point — and the comfort. From the posthumous Poems, 1633.

title: The Darkling Thrush
author: Thomas Hardy
lines: I leant upon a coppice gate
When Frost was spectre-grey,
And Winter's dregs made desolate
The weakening eye of day.
editorial_note: Dated to the last day of the nineteenth century, the poem finds one stubborn bird singing into the gloom. Hardy lets you decide whether that's hope or just habit. First published 1900.

title: Loveliest of Trees, the Cherry Now
author: A. E. Housman
lines: Loveliest of trees, the cherry now
Is hung with bloom along the bough,
And stands about the woodland ride
Wearing white for Eastertide.
editorial_note: A young man does the arithmetic of his own life against the blossom and decides to go look at it. Housman's gift was making mortality sound like good sense. From A Shropshire Lad, 1896.

title: We Wear the Mask
author: Paul Laurence Dunbar
lines: We wear the mask that grins and lies,
It hides our cheeks and shades our eyes,—
This debt we pay to human guile;
With torn and bleeding hearts we smile,
editorial_note: Dunbar names the cost of the smile the world demands — written by a Black American poet at the turn of the century, and exact about a hiding that was never voluntary. From Lyrics of Lowly Life, 1896.

title: Sympathy
author: Paul Laurence Dunbar
lines: I know what the caged bird feels, alas!
When the sun is bright on the upland slopes;
When the wind stirs soft through the springing grass,
And the river flows like a stream of glass;
editorial_note: The poem that gave Maya Angelou her title — Dunbar's caged bird sings not from joy but from a prayer it cannot stop making. From Lyrics of the Hearthside, 1899.

title: The Soldier
author: Rupert Brooke
lines: If I should die, think only this of me:
That there's some corner of a foreign field
That is for ever England. There shall be
In that rich earth a richer dust concealed;
editorial_note: Brooke's sonnet made the early war sound noble; he died of infection on the way to Gallipoli before he could see it turn otherwise. Read it knowing what the poets who followed would answer. From 1914 and Other Poems, 1915.

title: First Fig
author: Edna St. Vincent Millay
lines: My candle burns at both ends;
It will not last the night;
But ah, my foes, and oh, my friends—
It gives a lovely light!
editorial_note: Four lines that became the motto of a whole restless decade. Millay knew exactly how reckless it sounded, and meant every word. From A Few Figs from Thistles, 1920.

title: Sea Fever
author: John Masefield
lines: I must go down to the seas again, to the lonely sea and the sky,
And all I ask is a tall ship and a star to steer her by;
And the wheel's kick and the wind's song and the white sail's shaking,
And a grey mist on the sea's face, and a grey dawn breaking.
editorial_note: The pull of the horizon, set to a rhythm that rolls like the swell itself. Masefield had been to sea young, and the longing in it is the real thing. From Salt-Water Ballads, 1902.

title: The Eagle
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson
lines: He clasps the crag with crooked hands;
Close to the sun in lonely lands,
Ring'd with the azure world, he stands.
The wrinkled sea beneath him crawls;
editorial_note: A fragment, Tennyson called it, but few whole poems are this tightly made — every hard consonant clamps like a talon. A small one to type slowly. First published 1851.

title: O Captain! My Captain!
author: Walt Whitman
lines: O Captain! my Captain! our fearful trip is done,
The ship has weather'd every rack, the prize we sought is won,
The port is near, the bells I hear, the people all exulting,
While follow eyes the steady keel, the vessel grim and daring;
editorial_note: Whitman's elegy for Lincoln, and the one time this most free of poets reached for rhyme and refrain. He half-regretted its popularity; readers never have. First published 1865.

title: Kubla Khan
author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
lines: In Xanadu did Kubla Khan
A stately pleasure-dome decree:
Where Alph, the sacred river, ran
Through caverns measureless to man
editorial_note: Coleridge claimed he dreamed the whole poem on opium and lost the rest when a visitor knocked. Fragment or not, the opening is pure incantation. First published 1816.

title: How Do I Love Thee? (Sonnet 43)
author: Elizabeth Barrett Browning
lines: How do I love thee? Let me count the ways.
I love thee to the depth and breadth and height
My soul can reach, when feeling out of sight
For the ends of being and ideal grace.
editorial_note: Written in secret to Robert Browning during their courtship and slipped to him after they married. The counting never feels dutiful — it feels like wonder. From Sonnets from the Portuguese, 1850.

title: The Lamb
author: William Blake
lines: Little Lamb, who made thee?
Dost thou know who made thee?
Gave thee life, and bid thee feed,
By the stream and o'er the mead;
editorial_note: The gentle counterweight to Blake's Tyger — innocence asking the same question experience will later make terrifying. Best typed with both poems in mind. From Songs of Innocence, 1789.

title: Annabel Lee
author: Edgar Allan Poe
lines: It was many and many a year ago,
In a kingdom by the sea,
That a maiden there lived whom you may know
By the name of Annabel Lee;
editorial_note: Poe's last complete poem, a lullaby that turns into a haunting. The sing-song meter is a trap — it makes the grief easier to swallow and harder to forget. First published 1849.

title: A Psalm of Life
author: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
lines: Tell me not, in mournful numbers,
Life is but an empty dream!
For the soul is dead that slumbers,
And things are not what they seem.
editorial_note: The poem that taught a century to make its life sublime. Earnest to its bones — and a useful thing to type on a morning that needs a push. First published 1838.

title: The World Is Too Much with Us
author: William Wordsworth
lines: The world is too much with us; late and soon,
Getting and spending, we lay waste our powers;—
Little we see in Nature that is ours;
We have given our hearts away, a sordid boon!
editorial_note: Wordsworth's complaint about busyness and money is older than your phone and twice as sharp. A fitting one for a warm-up that asks you to slow down. From Poems, in Two Volumes, 1807.

title: Anthem for Doomed Youth
author: Wilfred Owen
lines: What passing-bells for these who die as cattle?
Only the monstrous anger of the guns.
Only the stuttering rifles' rapid rattle
Can patter out their hasty orisons.
editorial_note: The reply to every poem that called the war glorious — Owen heard church bells in artillery and refused to look away. He was killed a week before the Armistice. Published posthumously, 1920.

title: No Coward Soul Is Mine
author: Emily Brontë
lines: No coward soul is mine,
No trembler in the world's storm-troubled sphere:
I see Heaven's glories shine,
And faith shines equal, arming me from fear.
editorial_note: Said to be the last poem Emily Brontë wrote, and a wall of pure conviction. Charlotte published it after her sister's death, calling it her final word. First published 1850.

title: Requiem
author: Robert Louis Stevenson
lines: Under the wide and starry sky,
Dig the grave and let me lie.
Glad did I live and gladly die,
And I laid me down with a will.
editorial_note: Stevenson wrote his own epitaph years before he needed it; it's carved on his grave in Samoa. Few poems make peace sound this earned. From Underwoods, 1887.

title: Jabberwocky
author: Lewis Carroll
lines: 'Twas brillig, and the slithy toves
Did gyre and gimble in the wabe:
All mimsy were the borogoves,
And the mome raths outgrabe.
editorial_note: The greatest nonsense poem in English, and proof that meaning can ride entirely on rhythm and sound. Typing the invented words is its own strange pleasure. From Through the Looking-Glass, 1871.

title: Crossing the Bar
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson
lines: Sunset and evening star,
And one clear call for me!
And may there be no moaning of the bar,
When I put out to sea,
editorial_note: Tennyson asked that this always be printed last in his collections, and it usually is — a calm sailing-out written near the end of his life. First published 1889.

title: Bright Star
author: John Keats
lines: Bright star, would I were stedfast as thou art—
Not in lone splendour hung aloft the night
And watching, with eternal lids apart,
Like nature's patient, sleepless Eremite,
editorial_note: Keats wanted the star's constancy without its loneliness — to be steadfast and still in love. He copied it into a book of Shakespeare aboard the ship to Italy, where he would die. Published 1838.

title: Success Is Counted Sweetest
author: Emily Dickinson
lines: Success is counted sweetest
By those who ne'er succeed.
To comprehend a nectar
Requires sorest need.
editorial_note: Dickinson's clean little paradox: you understand a thing best by lacking it. One of the few poems published in her lifetime, here in the regularized 1890 edition.

title: The Listeners
author: Walter de la Mare
lines: 'Is there anybody there?' said the Traveller,
Knocking on the moonlit door;
And his horse in the silence champed the grasses
Of the forest's ferny floor:
editorial_note: A whole ghost story compressed into a question no one answers. De la Mare builds the hush so well you start listening too. First published 1912.

title: Dreams
author: Langston Hughes
lines: Hold fast to dreams
For if dreams die
Life is a broken-winged bird
That cannot fly.
editorial_note: Eight plain lines that a child can memorize and an adult can't forget. Hughes made the case for holding on without a wasted syllable. First published 1922.

title: If We Must Die
author: Claude McKay
lines: If we must die, let it not be like hogs
Hunted and penned in an inglorious spot,
While round us bark the mad and hungry dogs,
Making their mock at our accursed lot.
editorial_note: McKay wrote it during the violence of 1919's Red Summer — a sonnet that turns defiance into dignity. Its defiance has been borrowed by the cornered ever since. First published 1919.

title: Trees
author: Joyce Kilmer
lines: I think that I shall never see
A poem lovely as a tree.
A tree whose hungry mouth is prest
Against the earth's sweet flowing breast;
editorial_note: Sentimental, sincere, and beloved precisely for both — a poem that has outlived every critic who rolled their eyes at it. Kilmer was killed in the war five years later. First published 1913.

title: On Being Brought from Africa to America
author: Phillis Wheatley
lines: 'Twas mercy brought me from my Pagan land,
Taught my benighted soul to understand
That there's a God, that there's a Saviour too:
Once I redemption neither sought nor knew.
editorial_note: Wheatley was the first African American to publish a book of poetry — enslaved, and writing in a form that turns piety into a quiet argument for her own equality. From Poems on Various Subjects, 1773.

title: Ode on a Grecian Urn
author: John Keats
lines: Thou still unravish'd bride of quietness,
Thou foster-child of silence and slow time,
Sylvan historian, who canst thus express
A flowery tale more sweetly than our rhyme:
editorial_note: Keats addresses an urn as if it could answer, and that one-sided intimacy is the whole ache of the poem. Typing the opening apostrophe lets the long vowels slow your hands the way the marble slows time. Text follows the 1820 Lamia volume.

title: La Belle Dame sans Merci
author: John Keats
lines: O what can ail thee, knight-at-arms,
Alone and palely loitering?
The sedge has wither'd from the lake,
And no birds sing.
editorial_note: A ballad that turns enchantment into dread in four short stanzas. The clipped final line lands like a held breath. Text follows the 1820 version Keats published in the Indicator.

title: Ode to the West Wind
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
lines: O wild West Wind, thou breath of Autumn's being,
Thou, from whose unseen presence the leaves dead
Are driven, like ghosts from an enchanter fleeing,
Yellow, and black, and pale, and hectic red,
editorial_note: Shelley wrote this in a wood near Florence as a storm gathered, and the lines still move like weather. The list of colours in the fourth line is a small inventory of decay. Text follows the 1820 Prometheus Unbound volume.

title: London
author: William Blake
lines: I wander thro' each charter'd street,
Near where the charter'd Thames does flow,
And mark in every face I meet
Marks of weakness, marks of woe.
editorial_note: Blake repeats 'charter'd' and 'mark' until the words feel like manacles, which is the point. A short walk through the city becomes an indictment of it. Text follows Songs of Experience, 1794.

title: The Rime of the Ancient Mariner
author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
lines: It is an ancient Mariner,
And he stoppeth one of three.
'By thy long grey beard and glittering eye,
Now wherefore stopp'st thou me?
editorial_note: The poem opens by grabbing a wedding guest, and the reader, by the sleeve. That glittering eye is the first sign you will not be let go. Text follows the Lyrical Ballads, 1798.

title: So We'll Go No More a Roving
author: Lord Byron
lines: So, we'll go no more a roving
So late into the night,
Though the heart be still as loving,
And the moon be still as bright.
editorial_note: Byron sent these lines in a letter at twenty-eight, already weary of his own revels. The tenderness is in the things that stay bright while he stops. Text follows the 1830 Letters and Journals, where Thomas Moore first printed it.

title: Composed upon Westminster Bridge
author: William Wordsworth
lines: Earth has not anything to show more fair:
Dull would he be of soul who could pass by
A sight so touching in its majesty:
This City now doth, like a garment, wear
editorial_note: The great poet of mountains and lakes is stopped cold by London at dawn. The city is beautiful precisely because it is, for once, asleep and still. Text follows Poems, in Two Volumes, 1807.

title: Dover Beach
author: Matthew Arnold
lines: The sea is calm to-night.
The tide is full, the moon lies fair
Upon the straits; on the French coast the light
Gleams and is gone; the cliffs of England stand,
editorial_note: Arnold begins with a honeymoon view and slides, almost imperceptibly, toward the loss of faith. The light that gleams and is gone is the whole argument in miniature. Text follows New Poems, 1867.

title: Home-Thoughts, from Abroad
author: Robert Browning
lines: Oh, to be in England
Now that April's there,
And whoever wakes in England
Sees, some morning, unaware,
editorial_note: Homesickness rendered as a precise botanical longing for a specific month. Browning wrote it from Italy, which makes the ache earned. Text follows Dramatic Romances and Lyrics, 1845.

title: Ulysses
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson
lines: It little profits that an idle king,
By this still hearth, among these barren crags,
Match'd with an aged wife, I mete and dole
Unequal laws unto a savage race,
editorial_note: An old hero refuses to settle into retirement, and the restlessness is half-noble, half-selfish. Tennyson wrote it grieving a friend, which gives the striving its grief. Text follows Poems, 1842.

title: The Charge of the Light Brigade
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson
lines: Half a league, half a league,
Half a league onward,
All in the valley of Death
Rode the six hundred.
editorial_note: The galloping metre carries you forward exactly as the order carried the men, whether or not it made sense. Repetition becomes momentum becomes doom. Text follows the 1854 printing in the Examiner.

title: To His Coy Mistress
author: Andrew Marvell
lines: Had we but world enough, and time,
This coyness, lady, were no crime.
We would sit down, and think which way
To walk, and pass our long love's day.
editorial_note: The most charming bad argument in English, mounting a syllogism in favour of seizing the day. The wit only sharpens once mortality enters the room. Text follows the posthumous Miscellaneous Poems, 1681.

title: To the Virgins, to Make Much of Time
author: Robert Herrick
lines: Gather ye rosebuds while ye may,
Old Time is still a-flying;
And this same flower that smiles to-day
To-morrow will be dying.
editorial_note: The carpe diem poem so distilled that its first line became a proverb. Herrick's lightness is the sugar on a genuinely dark pill. Text follows Hesperides, 1648.

title: The Passionate Shepherd to His Love
author: Christopher Marlowe
lines: Come live with me and be my love,
And we will all the pleasures prove
That valleys, groves, hills, and fields,
Woods, or steepy mountain yields.
editorial_note: Pastoral seduction at its most golden, promising a world where it is always spring. Marlowe never lets reality, or rent, intrude. Text follows the 1599 printing in The Passionate Pilgrim.

title: Song: To Celia
author: Ben Jonson
lines: Drink to me only with thine eyes,
And I will pledge with mine;
Or leave a kiss but in the cup,
And I'll not look for wine.
editorial_note: Jonson builds a whole courtship out of glances and a shared cup, no wine required. It became a song precisely because the lines already hum. Text follows The Forest in his Works, 1616.

title: Virtue
author: George Herbert
lines: Sweet day, so cool, so calm, so bright,
The bridal of the earth and sky;
The dew shall weep thy fall to-night;
For thou must die.
editorial_note: Herbert praises the day, the rose, the spring, then quietly buries each one. The blunt refrain 'thou must die' is the hinge the whole poem turns on. Text follows The Temple, 1633.

title: On His Blindness
author: John Milton
lines: When I consider how my light is spent,
Ere half my days, in this dark world and wide,
And that one talent which is death to hide
Lodged with me useless, though my soul more bent
editorial_note: Milton, going blind, wrestles with whether God still wants his work, and arrives at patience. The famous resolution comes only after this anxious opening. Text follows the 1673 Poems.

title: Elegy Written in a Country Churchyard
author: Thomas Gray
lines: The curfew tolls the knell of parting day,
The lowing herd wind slowly o'er the lea,
The ploughman homeward plods his weary way,
And leaves the world to darkness and to me.
editorial_note: Gray turns an evening in a village graveyard into a meditation on every unrecorded life. The opening lines lower the light by degrees. Text follows the 1751 first edition.

title: Sonnet 75 (One Day I Wrote Her Name)
author: Edmund Spenser
lines: One day I wrote her name upon the strand,
But came the waves and washed it away:
Again I wrote it with a second hand,
But came the tide, and made my pains his prey.
editorial_note: Spenser writes a name on the beach, the sea erases it, and he argues poetry will outlast the tide. The small futile gesture sets up a large claim. Text follows Amoretti, 1595.

title: To My Dear and Loving Husband
author: Anne Bradstreet
lines: If ever two were one, then surely we.
If ever man were loved by wife, then thee;
If ever wife was happy in a man,
Compare with me, ye women, if you can.
editorial_note: America's first published poet writes plainly and stakes a confident claim on happiness. The 'if ever' anaphora dares anyone to match her. Text follows the posthumous 1678 Several Poems.

title: A Valediction: Forbidding Mourning
author: John Donne
lines: As virtuous men pass mildly away,
And whisper to their souls to go,
Whilst some of their sad friends do say
The breath goes now, and some say, No:
editorial_note: Donne tells his wife not to grieve their parting and reaches for the famous compass image to prove it. It opens, strikingly, with a deathbed. Text follows the posthumous Poems, 1633.

title: Abou Ben Adhem
author: Leigh Hunt
lines: Abou Ben Adhem (may his tribe increase!)
Awoke one night from a deep dream of peace,
And saw, within the moonlight in his room,
Making it rich, and like a lily in bloom,
editorial_note: A small parable that love of one's fellow men outranks even love of God. Hunt tells it with a fable's warmth and economy. Text follows the 1834 printing in the New Monthly Magazine.

title: They Are Not Long
author: Ernest Dowson
lines: They are not long, the weeping and the laughter,
Love and desire and hate:
I think they have no portion in us after
We pass the gate.
editorial_note: Dowson compresses a whole philosophy of transience into a few lines that lend Hollywood its phrase 'the days of wine and roses.' Brief by design. Text follows Verses, 1896.

title: If—
author: Rudyard Kipling
lines: If you can keep your head when all about you
Are losing theirs and blaming it on you,
If you can trust yourself when all men doubt you,
But make allowance for their doubting too;
editorial_note: A father's column of conditions, each 'if' withholding the reward until the very last line. Its steady cadence is the discipline it preaches. Text follows Rewards and Fairies, 1910.

title: When I Was One-and-Twenty
author: A. E. Housman
lines: When I was one-and-twenty
I heard a wise man say,
'Give crowns and pounds and guineas
But not your heart away;
editorial_note: Housman lets a young man ignore good advice and pay for it a stanza later. The lightness of the verse hides the bruise. Text follows A Shropshire Lad, 1896.

title: The Donkey
author: G. K. Chesterton
lines: When fishes flew and forests walked
And figs grew upon thorn,
Some moment when the moon was blood
Then surely I was born.
editorial_note: Chesterton gives the lowly donkey a voice, and saves its one moment of glory for the final stanza. The grotesque opening makes the ending land harder. Text follows The Wild Knight, 1900.

title: Dulce et Decorum Est
author: Wilfred Owen
lines: Bent double, like old beggars under sacks,
Knock-kneed, coughing like hags, we cursed through sludge,
Till on the haunting flares we turned our backs,
And towards our distant rest began to trudge.
editorial_note: Owen drags the reader through the mud to dismantle the old lie that it is sweet to die for one's country. The exhausted similes do the arguing. Text follows the posthumous Poems, 1920. A frank depiction of war.

title: Everyone Sang
author: Siegfried Sassoon
lines: Everyone suddenly burst out singing;
And I was filled with such delight
As prisoned birds must find in freedom,
Winging wildly across the white
editorial_note: Written at the Armistice, the poem lets relief arrive as uncontrollable song. After years of his war verse, the release is almost startling. Text follows Picture Show, 1919.

title: The Oxen
author: Thomas Hardy
lines: Christmas Eve, and twelve of the clock.
'Now they are all on their knees,'
An elder said as we sat in a flock
By the embers in hearthside ease.
editorial_note: Hardy recalls a childhood legend that beasts kneel at midnight on Christmas, and half-wishes he still believed it. Doubt and yearning sit by the same fire. Text follows the 1915 printing in The Times.

title: Silver
author: Walter de la Mare
lines: Slowly, silently, now the moon
Walks the night in her silver shoon;
This way, and that, she peers, and sees
Silver fruit upon silver trees;
editorial_note: Nearly every line places the word 'silver,' so the poem itself seems moonlit. De la Mare turns repetition into a hush. Text follows Peacock Pie, 1913.

title: Richard Cory
author: Edwin Arlington Robinson
lines: Whenever Richard Cory went down town,
We people on the pavement looked at him:
He was a gentleman from sole to crown,
Clean favored, and imperially slim.
editorial_note: Robinson builds an enviable man in three stanzas so the last line can pull the floor out. The admiring tone is the trap. Text follows The Children of the Night, 1897. The ending touches on suicide.

title: A Man Said to the Universe
author: Stephen Crane
lines: A man said to the universe:
'Sir, I exist!'
'However,' replied the universe,
'The fact has not created in me
editorial_note: Crane stages cosmic indifference as a flat little exchange, and the universe gets the better lines. Its modern dryness still startles. Text follows War Is Kind, 1899.

title: Lift Every Voice and Sing
author: James Weldon Johnson
lines: Lift every voice and sing,
Till earth and heaven ring,
Ring with the harmonies of Liberty;
Let our rejoicing rise
editorial_note: Written for a Lincoln's-birthday celebration and later called the Black national anthem. The verbs all reach upward. Text follows the 1900 lyric, later collected in 1922.

title: Yet Do I Marvel
author: Countee Cullen
lines: I doubt not God is good, well-meaning, kind,
And did He stoop to quibble could tell why
The little buried mole continues blind,
Why flesh that mirrors Him must some day die,
editorial_note: Cullen frames a sonnet of theological puzzles only to spring the real one in its final couplet. The classical form makes the closing turn sting. Text follows Color, 1925.

title: America
author: Claude McKay
lines: Although she feeds me bread of bitterness,
And sinks into my throat her tiger's tooth,
Stealing my breath of life, I will confess
I love this cultured hell that tests my youth.
editorial_note: McKay holds love and fury for the same country in a single breath and refuses to resolve them. The contradiction is the truth of it. Text follows the 1921 printing in the Liberator.

title: Mother to Son
author: Langston Hughes
lines: Well, son, I'll tell you:
Life for me ain't been no crystal stair.
It's had tacks in it,
And splinters,
editorial_note: Hughes gives a mother a plain extended metaphor of a battered staircase and her refusal to stop climbing. The voice carries everything. Text follows the 1922 printing in The Crisis.

title: I, Too
author: Langston Hughes
lines: I, too, sing America.
I am the darker brother.
They send me to eat in the kitchen
When company comes,
editorial_note: A quiet answer to Whitman that claims the table and the nation. Confidence builds line by line toward 'tomorrow.' Text follows The Weary Blues, 1926.

title: Recuerdo
author: Edna St. Vincent Millay
lines: We were very tired, we were very merry-
We had gone back and forth all night on the ferry.
It was bare and bright, and smelled like a stable-
But we looked into a fire, we leaned across a table,
editorial_note: Millay turns one giddy, sleepless night into a refrain of tiredness and joy. The repeated opening line rocks like the ferry itself. Text follows A Few Figs from Thistles, 1920.

title: Afternoon on a Hill
author: Edna St. Vincent Millay
lines: I will be the gladdest thing
Under the sun!
I will touch a hundred flowers
And not pick one.
editorial_note: A small vow of pure, uncomplicated happiness, all light and air. The choice not to pick is its quiet grace. Text follows Renascence and Other Poems, 1917.

title: The Red Wheelbarrow
author: William Carlos Williams
lines: so much depends
upon
a red wheel
barrow
editorial_note: Williams stakes everything on plain attention to an ordinary object, and the broken lines force you to look. Sixteen words, no capital letters, no metaphor. Text follows Spring and All, 1923.

title: In a Station of the Metro
author: Ezra Pound
lines: The apparition of these faces in the crowd;
Petals on a wet, black bough.
editorial_note: Pound pared a thirty-line draft down to two, and Imagism had its manifesto. The whole poem is one image laid against another. Text follows the 1913 printing in Poetry.

title: The Love Song of J. Alfred Prufrock
author: T. S. Eliot
lines: Let us go then, you and I,
When the evening is spread out against the sky
Like a patient etherized upon a table;
Let us go, through certain half-deserted streets,
editorial_note: Eliot opens a love song with an anaesthetised sky, and modern poetry's nervous self-consciousness arrives fully formed. The third line is the famous swerve. Text follows the 1915 printing in Poetry.

title: The Snow Man
author: Wallace Stevens
lines: One must have a mind of winter
To regard the frost and the boughs
Of the pine-trees crusted with snow;
And have been cold a long time
editorial_note: Stevens argues that to see winter truly you must empty yourself of feeling about it. The chilly logic is the poem's strange beauty. Text follows the 1921 printing in Poetry.

title: Fire and Ice
author: Robert Frost
lines: Some say the world will end in fire,
Some say in ice.
From what I've tasted of desire
I hold with those who favor fire.
editorial_note: Frost weighs apocalypse by fire against ice and maps them onto desire and hate, all with a shrug. Nine lines, casual and chilling. Text follows the 1920 printing in Harper's Magazine.

title: Nothing Gold Can Stay
author: Robert Frost
lines: Nature's first green is gold,
Her hardest hue to hold.
Her early leaf's a flower;
But only so an hour.
editorial_note: Frost watches the first gold of spring fade and folds Eden into eight lines. The tight rhymes pass as quickly as the thing they mourn. Text follows New Hampshire, 1923.

title: A Noiseless Patient Spider
author: Walt Whitman
lines: A noiseless patient spider,
I mark'd where on a little promontory it stood isolated,
Mark'd how to explore the vacant vast surrounding,
It launch'd forth filament, filament, filament, out of itself.
editorial_note: Whitman watches a spider fling out threads into emptiness and sees his own soul doing the same. The repeated 'filament' is the reaching made audible. Text follows the 1868 printing, later in Leaves of Grass.

title: The Second Coming
author: W. B. Yeats
lines: Turning and turning in the widening gyre
The falcon cannot hear the falconer;
Things fall apart; the centre cannot hold;
Mere anarchy is loosed upon the world,
editorial_note: Written after the Great War, the poem's images of disintegration have never stopped being quoted. The third line alone has become shorthand for collapse. Text follows the 1920 printing in The Dial.

title: He Wishes for the Cloths of Heaven
author: W. B. Yeats
lines: Had I the heavens' embroidered cloths,
Enwrought with golden and silver light,
The blue and the dim and the dark cloths
Of night and light and the half-light,
editorial_note: Yeats offers, having nothing, only his dreams, and asks that they be trodden on softly. The poverty is what makes the gift tender. Text follows The Wind Among the Reeds, 1899.

title: A Dream Within a Dream
author: Edgar Allan Poe
lines: Take this kiss upon the brow!
And, in parting from you now,
Thus much let me avow-
You are not wrong, who deem
editorial_note: Poe begins with a farewell and ends doubting whether anything was ever real, the sand running through his fingers. A parting that questions existence itself. Text follows the 1849 printing in the Flag of Our Union.

title: A Birthday
author: Christina Rossetti
lines: My heart is like a singing bird
Whose nest is in a watered shoot;
My heart is like an apple-tree
Whose boughs are bent with thick-set fruit;
editorial_note: Rossetti piles up images of abundance to match a joy that ordinary speech can't carry. The similes keep overflowing because the feeling does. Text follows Goblin Market and Other Poems, 1862.
